title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: What is the minimum integer $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$ such that every $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$-chromatic oriented graph has an acyclic $k$-chromatic subgraph?
context_text: Addario-Berry, Havet, Sales, Reed, and Thomassé observed that if a $k$-chromatic oriented graph has no directed cycle it contains every oriented tree of order $k$, and suggested that proving acyclic subgraphs of high chromatic number exist in $k$-chromatic oriented graphs could yield improved bounds towards Burr's conjecture. The trivial two-part partition gives $f(k)\leq k^{2}$, subsequently refined to $f(k)\leq k^{2}-2k+2$, but substantially improving this bound seems to require new ideas.
paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number

title: Problem 1.2 (Nassar–Yuster)
statement_text: What is the minimum integer $g\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$ such that every $g\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$-vertex tournament has an acyclic $k$-chromatic subgraph?
context_text: Nassar and Yuster introduced this tournament-restricted version of Problem 1.1, proved $k^{8/7}/4\leq g(k)\leq k^{2}-(2-1/\sqrt{2})k+2$, and further conjectured that $g(k)=o(k^{2})$. The paper under review resolves this conjecture in strong form.
paper_contribution: Proves $k^{4/3-o(1)}\leq g(k)\leq k^{9/5+o(1)}$, establishing $g(k)=o(k^{2})$ in strong form and simultaneously improving both the upper and lower bounds of Nassar and Yuster.
notes: The specific conjecture $g(k)=o(k^{2})$ appears in prose in the context immediately following Problem 1.2 and is the central target stated in the abstract.
paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number

title: Conjecture 4.1
statement_text: Every $n$-vertex tournament has an acyclic subgraph with chromatic number at least $n^{3/4-o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$. That is, $g\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)\leq k^{4/3+o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$.
context_text: Having established $k^{4/3-o(1)}\leq g(k)\leq k^{9/5+o(1)}$, the authors suspect the lower bound is closer to the truth and propose this conjecture. The lower bound is achieved via a randomized projective-plane construction, and the authors also wonder whether a simpler explicit construction on $n=q^{2}$ vertices attains the same bound.
paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number
